content:
First observe that the sequence \(M\) of positive integers not divisible by 3 is
\[
m_{1}=1,\;m_{2}=2,\;m_{3}=4,\;m_{4}=5,\;m_{5}=7,\;m_{6}=8,\;\dots
\]
One checks (for instance by the “skip–3” or by a simple floor‐formula) that
\[
m_i \;=\;i+\Big\lfloor\frac{i-1}{2}\Big\rfloor.
\]
We seek \(2n\) consecutive terms whose sum is 300:
\[
m_k + m_{k+1}+\cdots + m_{k+2n-1}\;=\;300.
\]
A direct summation (splitting into even/odd \(k\)) leads to the two Diophantine forms
\[
100 \;=\;n\,(2s+n)\quad\text{or}\quad
100 \;=\;n\,(2s+n-1),
\]
with \(s\) an integer and the obvious positivity constraints.  Checking all divisors \(n\mid100\) one finds exactly
\[
n\in\{1,2,4,5,10\}
\]
admit a (positive) solution \(s\).  Indeed for example
\[
n=1:\;m_{100}+m_{101}=149+151=300,\quad
n=2:\;m_{49}+m_{50}+m_{51}+m_{52}=73+74+76+77=300,
\]
etc.

Answer:\quad\boxed{n=1,2,4,5,\text{ or }10.}
